utterances: Every time a bacteria is exposed to an antibiotic but isn't killed by it, it has the potential to develop resistance.
So the more we use antibiotics, the more we're going to encourage the growth of these bacteria that are resistant to them.
Hospitals around the world increasingly have been reporting bacterial infections that don't respond to traditional antibiotics.
Then you have these extreme cases like the woman just - what?
- about a year ago who died of an infection in Nevada that was resistant to 26 different antibiotics.
So the bacteria are out there that are resistant to everything, and they are becoming more and more prevalent.
Health officials worry about so much growth in antibiotic use in low and middle-income countries because these drugs are often available there without a prescription.
So the potential for misuse is high.
Also, price notes that poor sanitation in impoverished nations adds to the problem.

If a superbug develops in a patient, for instance, who only has access to an outhouse, that bug is far more likely to spread and take on a life of its own than if that patient had access to a toilet connected to a sewage treatment plant.
Jason Beaubien, NPR News.
I'm Noah Adams.
This is DAY TO DAY.
The New York Daily News has been getting the inside scoop on stories big and small since it first launched back in 1919.
And now the new television reality show Tabloid Wars on Bravo is doing the same to the newspaper, pulling back the curtain on its inner workings.
Here is television critic Andrew Wallenstein.
Full disclosure, I am a newspaper reporter reviewing a show about newspaper reporters.
So forgive me if I came into Tabloid Wars a little skeptical about how interesting a reality show might be about my business.
Point a camera at my cubicle and all you're bound to see is cursing and coffee drinking.
Now Tabloid Wars isn't that uneventful, but that's about as strong a praise as I can offer.
Here's the newsflash: the people who cover the news aren't nearly as compelling as the news itself.
The show is called Tabloid Wars because its backdrop is the long rivalry between the New York Daily News and cross-town paper the New York Post, which is never seen but looms large in the show.
Daily News Editor-in-Chief Michael Cooke doesn't mince words when he assesses the competition.
There's a -
That was my first and last call to that number.
But four years ago, the bestseller list was packed with titles that included Bill Clinton's autobiography, John Dean's ""Worse Than Watergate: The Secret Presidency of George W.
Bush,"" Kevin Phillips' book on the Bush's called ""The Politics of Deceit,"" and several more titles in the same vein.
So making political predictions based on bestseller lists is probably like trying to put lipstick on a pig, just to coin a phrase.
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan, in Washington.
Most of us would like to think we'd do the right thing if we saw somebody breaking into a store or abusing a child.
But does informing the authorities about somebody tossing a cigarette out of their car make you a responsible citizen or a snitch?
In Iowa, there's a toll-free number for drivers to call when they see a fellow motorist empty an ashtray on the street or flip a burger bag out the window.
There is no fine, but the litterer gets letters from the Keep Iowa Beautiful organization, from the Iowa State Patrol chief, and from the heads of two police associations.
(Unintelligible), litterbugs are not the only miscreants under public scrutiny.

Parents in New York can attach a How's My Nanny sign on their kid's stroller so concerned passersby can jot down the plate number, log onto a Web site, and send an e-mail to the child's parents to report any inappropriate behavior.
And of course, there are the How's My Driving stickers on thousands of trucks and vans across the country.
Are these schemes useful tools to hold people more responsible for their actions or counterproductive invitations to rat?
Later in the program, home for the holidays, fixing the old folks computer.
But first, the snitch society.
Have you ever reported on a stranger?
We're not talking felonies in progress here but, if you will, quality of life crimes.
Have you ever been the recipient of an anonymous complaint?
The e-mail address is talk@npr.org.
And our first guest is Jill Starishevsky.
She launched the How's My Nanny license plates Web site in October.
She's a former prosecutor and the mother of two, and she joins us now from our bureau in New York.
Very nice of you to come in today.
Thank you so much for having me.
And why did you decide this was necessary?
Well, I decided it was necessary, I guess - let me just back up.
I'm a current prosecutor.
Oh.
I'm not a former - it's OK, I'm on maternity leave.
I just had my second child.
Congratulations.
Thank you.
I was in the park earlier this year back in March, and I observed two young children, little girls, playing by themselves, and they were unattended.
And I watched them for almost an hour and no one interacted with them.

No one made sure they weren't falling off of the jungle gym, no one made sure they didn't run into the street on Second Avenue, and no one made sure they weren't taken away at the hand of a stranger, which is where - I'm not only a prosecutor, but I prosecute sex crimes and child abuse and, unfortunately, most often sex crimes against children.
So when I saw these kids unattended, I'm thinking they could be sitting across from my desk as a victim of a child sex crime.
I then, a little while later, after an hour, noticed that they ran over to a woman in the park who turned out to be their nanny.
And I decided I was going to tell the parents about how their nanny wasn't watching the kids and they could have gotten seriously injured.
And I realized how am I going to figure out who the parents are?
And I wasn't able to, and this is why I created a way to do that.
Mm-hmm, and how - have a lot of people signed up for this?
We've had a lot of interest.
I also have to - I'm sorry to correct another thing - it's not just parents in New York.
We've already branched out to New Jersey.
We've gotten phone calls and e-mails from Vancouver.
We were on the phone on Friday with an affiliate who wants to start up in London.
So this is really - it's pervasive.
Moms and dads are worried about their children, and this is not to say that nannies are bad.
Of course there are wonderful nannies out there.
But this is just so that - it's three things.
One is so that the parents can have the reassurance that they need when they go back to work, so they can feel I'm doing everything possible to know that my child is OK.
Some people get nanny cams for inside the home, this is for when they're outside of the home they can know, you know, I know if something's going to happen, good or bad, it'll be reported to me.
That's the first thing.
The second thing is this is actually like a deterrent.
You know, it's funny I know you're talking about the whole how's my driving.

I had previously read when I was doing this earlier that, you know, 20 percent - when the trucking industry came out with these how am I driving bumper stickers - there was a 20 to, I think, 50 percent decrease of truck-related accidents, as a result, because people know that they are now reportable.
If this somehow stops a nanny from who might otherwise ignore a child or let them cry in a stroller, isn't that enough of a reason to do it?
And then finally, there's the reporting.
You can get a negative report saying, you know, your nanny wasn't doing something or your nanny did something bad; you should know what it is.
And you can get a wonderful, positive report.
The site is set up so that the nannies can receive praise, and how else are they going to get praise because the parents don't know what they're doing all day.
So it's really beneficial on all of those levels.
It's not necessarily the nanny who may be pushing that stroller that day.
It might be the mom or the dad.
Interesting.
When I first came up with this idea, I thought, you know what, I'll just make a little pin.
It'll say how's my nanny?
We'll stick it to the diaper bag, and that'll be it.
Well, I think there can be less question of that now.
The United States' larger strategy with respect to Syria in the region remains to be articulated beyond the need to defeat and destroy ISIS.
The Turks continue to see the strategic problem in different terms but now very much including the need to defeat ISIS.
So there's more common ground than there was before, and it's more overt and explicit.
Is there some risk that the U.S., though, winds up losing Kurdish fighters who have really proven themselves successful against ISIS?
I don't see any trend in that direction.
I don't see the United States relaxing in the support of the Kurdish fighters on the ground against ISIS.
Why should that be?
Remember, the Turkish government is stating that it's facing a terrorist threat on many fronts, not only ISIS but also the PKK.
In that environment, where they're identifying numerous threats, the PKK are standing out as, once again, a threat.

That's really too bad because the government of Turkey and the PKK had been in what the Turks called a process - not to call it a peace process but just a process - of coming to terms to end the decades-old PKK-led insurgency, basically, against the Turkish state.
But the Kurds of Syria have explicitly taken themselves out of that conflict, the Kurdish-Turkish conflict.
What difference do you think Turkey's military might make in the fight against ISIS?
I think it could make a quite a large one in acting all the more effectively to seal off the border against others who try to go across it.
If they do get involved in intelligence collection or even kinetic action against ISIS targets, that could be very meaningful.
Basically for ISIS to have won itself another overt, explicit, powerful Muslim majority state as an enemy is quite a setback for ISIS.
Francis Ricciardone is the former U.S.
ambassador to Turkey.
Thanks so much for being with us.
My pleasure.
Thank you, Scott.
This is NEWS & NOTES.
I'm Farai Chideya.
